does: This paper provides a practitioner-focused examination of red-teaming and blue-teaming strategies for securing generative AI systems, particularly large language models deployed in production. The core contribution is the Attack Atlas, a practical framework designed to systematically analyze and categorize single-turn input attacks against GenAI systems. The work documents key vulnerabilities specific to natural language and multi-modal generative systems, identifies recurring challenges in adversarial threat assessment, and catalogs open questions that arise when translating academic adversarial robustness insights into real-world security evaluation. Rather than proposing novel attack methods, the paper synthesizes existing knowledge into actionable guidance grounded in deployment experience.
connects: This work builds on growing academic interest in adversarial robustness for large language models and extends prior research in adversarial NLP attacks to the production security context. It responds to the expanding literature on jailbreaking, prompt injection, and other input-based vulnerabilities in LLMs by providing a structured methodology for practitioners. The paper connects to established red-teaming and blue-teaming concepts from cybersecurity while adapting them specifically for generative AI's unique attack surface. It also engages with recent work on evaluating and benchmarking the safety and robustness of foundation models, acknowledging limitations in how academic findings translate to real-world threat modeling and defense strategies.
enables: This framework enables practitioners to systematically assess vulnerability landscapes in production GenAI systems and develop targeted defense strategies informed by structured attack categorization. The Attack Atlas provides a foundation for building more comprehensive security evaluation pipelines and helps prioritize mitigation efforts by organizing attacks into coherent threat categories. The work highlights open problems in defense evaluation—such as measuring robustness under distribution shift and testing adversarial resilience at scale—that future research can address. By bridging academic insights and practical security needs, it creates pathways for developing better red-teaming methodologies, more effective blue-team defenses, and standardized evaluation frameworks for assessing GenAI security in production environments.
